security_deposit.cap_rule: 1 month's rent (cap applies only to funds explicitly called 'security deposits')
security_deposit.special_rules_local_overrides_note: Local ordinances and special housing programs can add stricter rules.

eviction.nonpayment_notice_type: Varies — confirm required notice form (pay-or-quit, demand for possession, etc.).
eviction.lease_violation_notice_type: Varies — confirm notice type and cure rules.
eviction.court_process_summary_rule: Tex. Prop. Code § 24.005. NOTICE TO VACATE. (a) If the occupant is a tenant under a written lease or oral rental agreement, the landlord must give a tenant who defaults or holds over beyond a rental agreement three days' written notice to vacate the premises before the landlord files a forcible detainer suit, unless the parties have contracted for a shorter or longer notice period in a written lease or agreement. The landlord may not waive or eliminate the right to file a forcible detainer suit by accepting past due rent.
eviction.filing_court_rule: Tex. Prop. Code § 24.005. NOTICE TO VACATE. (a) If the occupant is a tenant under a written lease or oral rental agreement, the landlord must give a tenant who defaults or holds over beyond a rental agreement three days' written notice to vacate the premises before the landlord files a forcible detainer suit, unless the parties have contracted for a shorter or longer notice period in a written lease or agreement. The landlord may not waive or eliminate the right to file a forcible detainer suit by accepting past due rent.
eviction.service_of_process_rule: Tex. Prop. Code § 24.005. NOTICE TO VACATE. (a) If the occupant is a tenant under a written lease or oral rental agreement, the landlord must give a tenant who defaults or holds over beyond a rental agreement three days' written notice to vacate the premises before the landlord files a forcible detainer suit, unless the parties have contracted for a shorter or longer notice period in a written lease or agreement. The landlord may not waive or eliminate the right to file a forcible detainer suit by accepting past due rent.

landlord_entry.allowed_entry_reasons_rule: Tex. Prop. Code Sec. 92.0081. REMOVAL OF PROPERTY AND EXCLUSION OF RESIDENTIAL TENANT. (a) A landlord may not remove a door, window, or attic hatchway cover or a lock, latch, hinge, hinge pin, doorknob, or other mechanism connected to a door, window, or attic hatchway cover from a dwelling unit or remove furniture, fixtures, or appliances furnished by the landlord from a dwelling unit unless the landlord is making a good faith effort to repair or replace [them]. (b) Except as provided by Subsection (g), a landlord may not intentionally prevent a tenant from entering the leased premises except by judicial process.
landlord_entry.penalty_for_illegal_entry_rule: Varies — confirm remedies (injunction, damages, attorney fees) under state law.

rent_increase.cap_rule: No statewide rent control; local rules may apply.
rent_increase.fixed_term_increase_allowed_rule: Varies — confirm your lease terms and statute.
rent_increase.month_to_month_increase_allowed_rule: Varies — generally allowed with proper notice.
rent_increase.delivery_method_rule: Varies — confirm written notice and delivery method requirements.
rent_increase.retaliation_protection_note: Retaliation protections may limit rent increases after protected tenant complaints; confirm local/state rules.

habitability.landlord_repair_duty_rule: Varies — confirm landlord repair/habitability duties in statute and local code.
habitability.tenant_notice_required_rule: Varies — confirm required notice/cure steps before remedies.
habitability.tenant_termination_for_unrepaired_condit: Varies — some states allow termination after notice and failure to repair.
habitability.code_enforcement_contact_rule: Varies — contact local housing/code enforcement (city/county) for inspections and official repair orders.

sources.title: Tex. Prop. Code Sec. 92.052. LANDLORD'S DUTY TO REPAIR OR REMEDY. (a) A landlord shall make a diligent effort to repair or remedy a condition if: (1) the tenant specifies the condition in a notice to the person to whom or to the place where rent is normally paid; (2) the tenant is not delinquent in the payment of rent at the time notice is given; and (3) the condition: (A) materially affects the physical health or safety of an ordinary tenant; or (B) arises from the landlord's failure to provide and maintain in good working order a device to supply hot water of a minimum temperature of 120 degrees Fahrenheit.
extractedQuotes: Tex. Prop. Code Sec. 92.052. LANDLORD'S DUTY TO REPAIR OR REMEDY. (a) A landlord shall make a diligent effort to repair or remedy a condition if: (1) the tenant specifies the condition in a notice to the person to whom or to the place where rent is normally paid; (2) the tenant is not delinquent in the payment of rent at the time notice is given; and (3) the condition: (A) materially affects the physical health or safety of an ordinary tenant; or (B) arises from the landlord's failure to provide and maintain in good working order a device to supply hot water of a minimum temperature of 120 degrees Fahrenheit.
